title: 26th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2016 — Écolier (Grades 3-4)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: Amy, Bert, Carl, Doris and Ernst each rolled two dice and added the number of dots. Who rolled the largest total?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the dice images. Reading the dice: Amy=6+1=7, Bert=2+4=6, Carl=1+4=5, Doris=5+5=10, Ernst=6+6=12. Ernst rolled the largest total of 12.
solution_steps: Count the dots on each pair of dice from the figure.
The largest total is 12, rolled by Ernst.

questions.question_text: Small Kanga is 7 weeks and 2 days old. In how many days will Kanga be 8 weeks old?
questions.solution: Kanga is currently 7 weeks and 2 days = 49+2 = 51 days old. 8 weeks = 56 days. Days remaining = 56−51 = 5.
solution_steps: Current age: 7 weeks × 7 days + 2 days = 49 + 2 = 51 days.
Target age: 8 weeks = 8 × 7 = 56 days.
Days until 8 weeks: 56 − 51 = 5 days.

questions.question_text: The diagram shows: 17+3 flows into a box, and 20−16 flows into another box. These two boxes are added together to give ?. What is the value of ?
questions.solution: First box: 17+3=20. Second box: 20−16=4. Sum: 20+4=24.
solution_steps: Left branch: 17 + 3 = 20.
Right branch: 20 − 16 = 4.
Final sum: 20 + 4 = 24.

questions.question_text: What does Pipo see when he looks at himself in the mirror?

options.A: See PDF page 2 — option A image
options.B: See PDF page 2 — option B image
options.C: See PDF page 2 — option C image
options.D: See PDF page 2 — option D image
options.E: See PDF page 2 — option E image

questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the figure. A mirror reflection flips the image left-to-right. Option A shows the correct horizontal mirror image of Pipo.
solution_steps: A mirror reflection swaps left and right sides of the image.
Identify which option correctly mirrors Pipo's figure horizontally.
Option A is the correct mirror image.

questions.question_text: Geoff goes with his father to a circus. Their seats are numbered 71 and 72. Which way should they go? (The sign shows: seats 1–20 up arrow, seats 21–40 right arrow, seats 41–60 left arrow, seats 61–80 diagonal up-right arrow, seats 81–100 diagonal down-right arrow.)
questions.solution: Seats 71 and 72 fall in the 61–80 range, which corresponds to the diagonal up-right arrow (option D).
solution_steps: Find which range contains seats 71 and 72: the sign shows seats 61–80 go in the diagonal up-right direction.
Therefore they should follow the diagonal up-right arrow, option D.

questions.question_text: Anna shares some apples between herself and 5 friends. Everyone gets half of an apple. How many apples does she share?
questions.solution: There are 6 people in total (Anna + 5 friends). Each gets 1/2 an apple. Total apples = 6 × 1/2 = 3.
solution_steps: Total people: Anna + 5 friends = 6 people.
Each person gets half an apple: 6 × (1/2) = 3 apples.
Anna shares 3 apples.

questions.question_text: A rectangle is partly hidden behind a curtain. What shape is the hidden part?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the figure. The curtain is draped diagonally over one corner of the rectangle, leaving a triangular portion hidden. The hidden shape is a right triangle.
solution_steps: The rectangle is partially visible; the curtain covers one corner diagonally.
The hidden portion has three sides: two sides of the rectangle and the diagonal curtain edge.
Three sides means the hidden part is a triangle.

questions.question_text: Which one of the following sentences correctly describes the picture? (The picture shows a collection of circles, triangles, and squares.)

options.A: There are as many circles as squares.
options.B: There are fewer circles than triangles.
options.C: There are twice as many circles as triangles.
options.D: There are more squares than triangles.
options.E: There are two triangles more than circles.

questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the figure. Counting: 4 circles, 2 triangles, 2 squares. There are exactly twice as many circles (4) as triangles (2).
solution_steps: Count each shape in the picture: 4 circles, 2 triangles, 2 squares.
Check option C: twice as many circles as triangles → 4 = 2 × 2. True.
Verify others: A: 4≠2 (no); B: 4<2 (no); D: 2>2 (no); E: 2>4 (no). Only C is correct.

questions.question_text: The sum of the digits of the year 2016 is equal to 9. What is the next year, after 2016, where the sum of the digits of the year is equal to 9 again?
questions.solution: Check years after 2016: 2+0+1+7=10, 2+0+1+8=11, ..., 2+0+2+5=9. The next year with digit sum 9 is 2025.
solution_steps: The next year with digit sum 9 is 2025.

questions.question_text: The mouse wants to escape from the maze. How many different paths can the mouse take without passing through the same gate more than once?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the maze figure. Systematically tracing all valid paths from the mouse's starting position to the exit without reusing any gate yields exactly 4 distinct paths.
solution_steps: See PDF page 4 for the maze diagram.
Trace all paths from start to exit, never reusing a gate (edge).
Counting all valid routes gives 4 distinct paths.

questions.question_text: Zoe has two cards. She wrote a number on both sides of each card. The sum of the two numbers on the first card is equal to the sum of the numbers on the second card. The sum of the four numbers is 32. The visible sides show 5 and 12. What could be the two numbers on the sides that we cannot see?
questions.solution: Total sum = 32, so each card sums to 16. Card with visible 5: hidden side = 16−5=11. Card with visible 12: hidden side = 16−12=4. The hidden numbers are 11 and 4.
solution_steps: Both cards have the same sum, and total of all 4 numbers = 32, so each card sums to 32÷2 = 16.
Card 1 shows 5: hidden side = 16−5 = 11.
Card 2 shows 12: hidden side = 16−12 = 4.
The two hidden numbers are 11 and 4.

questions.question_text: Which tile fits in the middle such that only lines with the same colour touch each other?

options.A: See PDF page 4 — option A tile
options.B: See PDF page 4 — option B tile
options.C: See PDF page 4 — option C tile
options.D: See PDF page 4 — option D tile
options.E: See PDF page 4 — option E tile

questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the hexagonal tile puzzle. Option B is the only tile whose coloured lines correctly continue the surrounding pattern so that only same-coloured lines meet at each edge.
solution_steps: See PDF page 4 for the tile arrangement.
Check each edge of the central gap: identify the colour of the line entering from each surrounding tile.
Option B is the only tile that matches all six edges with the correct colour.

questions.question_text: Five children had a paper square, a paper triangle and a paper circle. Every child placed their own papers in a pile, as shown in the pictures. How many children placed the triangle above the square?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the five piles. Examining each child's arrangement, three of the five have the triangle higher in the stack than the square.
solution_steps: See PDF page 4 for the five stacking arrangements.
For each pile, determine whether the triangle appears above (on top of or higher than) the square.
Three out of five arrangements have triangle above square.

questions.question_text: Which three of the five jigsaw pieces shown can be joined together to form a square? The pieces are V (orange triangle), Z (green rectangle), U (red rectangle), X (light blue right triangle), Y (yellow triangle).
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the jigsaw pieces. Pieces V (small orange right triangle), X (large blue right triangle), and Y (small yellow right triangle) can be fitted together to form a complete square.
solution_steps: See PDF page 4 for the shapes of pieces V, X, and Y.
X is a large right triangle; V and Y are smaller right triangles.
Arranging V and Y to fill the corner cut of X creates a perfect square.

questions.question_text: Loes has started to write some numbers in the table. She decides that each row and column will contain the numbers 1, 2 and 3 exactly once. The 3×3 grid shows: top-left=1, middle-middle=2, middle-right=A, bottom-right=B. What is the sum of the numbers that she will write in squares A and B?
questions.solution: Each row and column has 1, 2, 3 exactly once. Column 2 has 2 in row 2, so rows 1 and 3 get 3 and 1 respectively. Row 1 then has 1, 3, 2. Row 2: needs 3 in column 1, so A=1. Row 3: column 1=2, column 2=1, so B=3. A+B=1+3=4.
solution_steps: Column 2 already has 2 in row 2, so (1,2)=3 and (3,2)=1.
Row 2: ?, 2, A → column 1 needs 3 (since col 1 has 1 in row 1), so (2,1)=3, A=1.

questions.question_text: John has a board with 11 squares. He puts a coin in each of eight neighbouring squares without leaving any empty squares between the coins. What is the maximum number of squares in which one can be sure that there is a coin?
questions.solution: The 8 consecutive coins can start at position 1, 2, 3, or 4. The squares guaranteed to have a coin regardless of placement are those in the intersection: positions 4 through 8, which is 5 squares.
solution_steps: The block of 8 coins can occupy positions 1–8, 2–9, 3–10, or 4–11.
Find the intersection of all four ranges: {1–8} ∩ {2–9} ∩ {3–10} ∩ {4–11} = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}.
That is 5 squares that are guaranteed to always contain a coin.

questions.question_text: Having turned a card over around its right side, we see what is drawn in the figure (a half-disc shape). What shall we see if we turn this card over around its upper side?

options.A: See PDF page 5 — option A image
options.B: See PDF page 5 — option B image
options.C: See PDF page 5 — option C image
options.D: See PDF page 5 — option D image
options.E: See PDF page 5 — option E image

questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the figure. Flipping around the right side mirrors left-right. Flipping around the upper side mirrors top-bottom. Option D shows the result of a top-bottom flip of the front face.
solution_steps: After the right-side flip, the back shows the given image (half-disc in lower-right).
Flipping around the upper side reflects the front face top-to-bottom.
Option D correctly shows the result of this flip.

questions.question_text: Tim, Tom and Jim are triplets (three brothers born on the same day). Their brother Paul is exactly 3 years older. Which of the following numbers can be the sum of the ages of the four brothers?
questions.solution: Let the triplets' age be x. Paul's age is x+3. Sum = 3x + (x+3) = 4x+3. Testing 27: 4x+3=27 → x=6, Paul=9. All ages are valid positive integers. No other option works: 25→x=5.5, 29→x=6.5, 30→x=6.75, 60→x=14.25.
solution_steps: Let triplets' age = x, Paul's age = x+3.
Total sum = 3x + (x+3) = 4x + 3.
25: 4x=22 → x=5.5 (not integer). 29: 4x=26 → x=6.5 (not integer). 30: 4x=27 → not integer. 60: 4x=57 → not integer.
Only 27 is achievable.

questions.question_text: Magic trees grow in a magic garden. Each tree contains either 6 pears and 3 apples or 8 pears and 4 apples. There are 25 apples in the garden. How many pears are there in the garden?
questions.solution: Let a = type-1 trees (6 pears, 3 apples), b = type-2 trees (8 pears, 4 apples). Then 3a+4b=25. Valid solutions: (a=7,b=1)→pears=42+8=50; (a=3,b=4)→pears=18+32=50. In all cases, pears=50.
solution_steps: Set up: 3a + 4b = 25 (apple equation).
Find non-negative integer solutions: (a=7, b=1) and (a=3, b=4).
Regardless of the tree combination, there are always 50 pears.

questions.question_text: My dogs have 18 more legs than noses. How many dogs do I have?
questions.solution: Each dog has 4 legs and 1 nose, so each dog contributes 3 more legs than noses. If there are n dogs: 3n=18, so n=6.
solution_steps: Each dog: 4 legs and 1 nose → 3 more legs than noses per dog.
Total extra legs: 3 × n = 18.

questions.question_text: In a basketball game a team has made a total of 72 shots (containing 1 point shots, 2 points shots and 3 points shots) and it finished the game scoring 99 points. We know that the team has obtained 30 baskets of 2 points, 12 of 1 point and some of 3 points. Which is the total number of shots that failed?
questions.solution: Points from 2-pt baskets: 30×2=60. Points from 1-pt baskets: 12×1=12. Points from 3-pt baskets: 99−60−12=27, so 27÷3=9 successful 3-pt shots. Total successful shots: 30+12+9=51. Failed shots: 72−51=21.
solution_steps: Successful 2-pt shots: 30, scoring 60 points.
Successful 1-pt shots: 12, scoring 12 points.
Remaining points: 99−60−12=27 from 3-pt shots → 27÷3=9 successful 3-pt shots.
Total successful shots: 30+12+9=51.
Failed shots: 72−51=21.

questions.question_text: Rachel adds seven numbers and gets 2016. One of the numbers in the addition is 201. She replaces the number 201 with 102. What answer does she get?
questions.solution: Replacing 201 with 102 decreases the sum by 201−102=99. New sum = 2016−99=1917.
solution_steps: The change: 102 − 201 = −99.

questions.question_text: Malte has built a bar of 27 bricks. He breaks the bar into two bars such that one of them is twice the length of the other. Then he takes one of the new bars and breaks it the same way. He continues in this way. Which of the following bars will he not be able to get?
questions.solution: Each break splits a bar in ratio 1:2. Starting from 27=3³, all obtainable lengths are of the form 3^a × 2^b where a≤3. Possible lengths: 1,2,3,4,6,8,9,12,18,27. The number 10 cannot be expressed in this form and is impossible to obtain.
solution_steps: Starting with 27, each break produces pieces of size n/3 and 2n/3.
All obtainable lengths are 27 × (1/3)^a × (2/3)^b = 3^(3-a) × 2^(b-a) for valid a,b, which simplifies to 3^p × 2^q with p+q ≤ 3 and p≥0, q≥0.
10 = 2×5 requires a factor of 5, which never appears. Answer: 10.

questions.question_text: Five sparrows sit on a branch: Angel, Bertha, Charlie, David, Eglio. Each sparrow chirps the same number of times as the number of sparrows it sees. Angel chirps four times. Then, one sparrow turns to look in the opposite direction. Again, each sparrow chirps the same number of times as the number of sparrows it sees. This time, the total number of chirps is more than the first time. Which of the sparrows has turned to look in the opposite direction?
questions.solution: Angel sees 4, so Angel faces toward the others. From the figure, Bertha faces left (away from the group to her right). Initially: Angel(right)=4, Bertha(left)=1, Charlie(right)=2, David(right)=1, Eglio(right)=0; total=8. When Bertha turns right, she now sees 3 sparrows instead of 1; total increases to 10. Only Bertha's turn increases the total.
solution_steps: Angel chirps 4 times → sees all 4 other sparrows → faces right.
From the figure, Bertha faces left (seeing only Angel = 1 sparrow). Others face right.
Initial totals: Angel=4, Bertha=1, Charlie=2, David=1, Eglio=0; sum=8.
If Bertha turns right: she now sees Charlie, David, Eglio = 3 sparrows. Change: +2. New total=10 > 8.
Only Bertha's turn increases the total chirp count.
